stem: Let \( A \) be a set with \( [[k]] \) elements and \( B \) be a set with \( [[n]] \) elements. How many injective (one-to-one) functions are there from \( A \) to \( B \)?
solution: An injective function sends each of the \( [[k]] \) elements of \( A \) to a DISTINCT element of \( B \), so there are \( [[n]] \) choices for the first, \( [[n]]-1 \) for the second, and so on down to \( [[n]]-[[k]]+1 \) choices for the last: \( \dfrac{[[n]]!}{([[n]]-[[k]])!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the count of ALL functions \( A \to B \), without requiring injectivity.
This divides by \( [[k]]! \), which would be correct for counting subsets of size \( [[k]] \) of \( B \), not for counting injective FUNCTIONS (order of assignment matters).
This only counts ways to permute the elements of \( A \) among themselves and ignores \( B \).
This uses one fewer factor in the denominator than the correct falling-factorial formula.
This swaps which set's size is the base and which is the exponent.
This divides the correct count by an unrelated factor of \( [[k]]! \).

stem: Let \( A \) and \( B \) each be sets with \( [[n]] \) elements. How many bijections (one-to-one correspondences) are there from \( A \) to \( B \)?
solution: A bijection between two \( [[n]] \)-element sets is exactly an arrangement (permutation) of the \( [[n]] \) elements of \( B \) to match up with the \( [[n]] \) elements of \( A \): there are \( [[n]]! = [[answer]] \) such correspondences.
distractor_rationales: This counts ALL functions \( A \to B \) (not requiring the map to be one-to-one or onto).
This is the number of subsets of an \( [[n]] \)-element set, unrelated to counting bijections.
This omits the last factor of the permutation count, as if only \( [[n]]-1 \) elements needed to be placed.
This only accounts for placing the first two elements and stops there.
This divides the correct count by an extra factor of \( [[n]] \).
This adds an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) to the correct count.

stem: How many surjective (onto) functions are there from a set of \( [[n]] \) elements to a set of \( [[m]] \) elements?
solution: By inclusion-exclusion on the \( [[m]] \) codomain elements that could be MISSED, the number of onto functions is \( \sum_{i=0}^{[[m]]} (-1)^i \binom{[[m]]}{i} ([[m]]-i)^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the count of ALL functions from the \( [[n]] \)-element set to the \( [[m]] \)-element set, without requiring every codomain element to be hit.
This applies the injective-function (falling factorial) formula, which needs the codomain to be at least as large as the domain here, and does not count onto-ness.
This confuses choosing a partition of the domain with a direct multiplication by \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[m]]} \), which double counts and undercounts different ways.
This swaps which set's size is the base and which is the exponent.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) to the correct count.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of \( [[m]] \) from the correct count.

stem: How many functions from a set of \( [[n]] \) elements to a set of \( [[m]] \) elements FAIL to be injective (one-to-one)?
solution: There are \( [[m]]^{[[n]]} \) functions from the \( [[n]] \)-element set to the \( [[m]] \)-element set in total, and \( \dfrac{[[m]]!}{([[m]]-[[n]])!} \) of them are injective. The number that are NOT injective is the complement: \( [[m]]^{[[n]]} - \dfrac{[[m]]!}{([[m]]-[[n]])!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract off the injective functions and just gives the total count.
This IS the count of injective functions, the complement of what was asked.
This swaps which set's size is the base and which is the exponent in the total-function count.
This subtracts the count of permutations of the domain instead of the true injective-function count.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of \( [[n]] \) to the correct count.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of \( [[m]] \) from the correct count.
